data.preview_translation:
Sharing my approach to managing Skills, with a geeky flair—not necessarily for everyone, but it can give folks an idea to work with.

I. Skills Only Installed in Projects, Not Globally

An Agent's Skills can be installed globally (shared across all projects) or within a specific
data.translation:
Sharing my approach to managing Skills, with a geeky flair—not necessarily for everyone, but it can give folks an idea to work with.

I. Skills Only Installed in Projects, Not Globally

An Agent's Skills can be installed globally (shared across all projects) or within a specific project (only usable in the current project). I opt to install them only within projects, mainly to save on context space.

When an Agent is working, it has a context window—you can think of it as Claude's workbench, with a limited surface area. While Skills load only their name, description, and other summary info by default (without sprawling out the full content), it adds up over time—installing dozens of Skills globally means even just those summaries will eat up a chunk of workbench space. Plus, once Claude deems a Skill relevant to the current task, it'll load the full content; the more global Skills you have, the higher the chance of accidental triggers, wasting space for nothing.

By installing only the truly needed Skills within a project, the workbench only holds the materials you actually use right now, leaving precious space for more important stuff, so Claude works more efficiently.

II. Use Soft Links to Install Skills

This is the core of my management method—first, a quick explainer on what soft links are.

You can think of a soft link as Windows' shortcut—there's only one actual file body, but you can create shortcuts pointing to it from tons of places. Change the original, and all shortcuts update in sync.

My specific routine breaks down into three steps:

Step 1: Download open-source Skills projects to a unified directory

I've got a dedicated folder on my computer for GitHub projects, ~/GitHub, where all downloaded open-source stuff lives, like:

~/GitHub/baoyu-skills      ← Open-source project holding various Skills
~/GitHub/baoyu-design      ← Another open-source project

This folder's like a repository—all the original Skills files are stored here.

Step 2: Create soft links in your own projects

Say I've got a writing project at ~/GitHub/baoyu-writing that needs several Skills. I don't copy them in—instead, I create soft links so the project points to the originals in the repo:

Path inside the project                  →  Actual target location (original)
.agents/skills/baoyu-comic          →  ~/GitHub/baoyu-skills/skills/baoyu-comic
.agents/skills/baoyu-design         →  ~/GitHub/baoyu-design/skills/baoyu-design

Step 3: Build an entry point for Claude Code

Finally, create a soft link so Claude Code can find these Skills:

.claude/skills  →  .agents/skills

Now Claude Code can follow this chain to all the needed Skills.

III. No Need to Memorize Commands—Let the Agent Handle It

By now you might be thinking: What if I can't remember the soft link commands?

No sweat at all. Just tell Codex/Claude Code what you want in plain natural language, like:

> Help me soft-link ~/GitHub/baoyu-skills/skills/baoyu-comic to .agents/skills/baoyu-comic

Or even simpler:

Link the baoyu-comic skill from the baoyu-skills project to the current project

The Agent will automatically create the soft link for you, and you can hand off all the ongoing maintenance, additions, and deletions to it too. Just spell out clearly which Skill to link where, and let the Agent sweat the gritty details.

IV. Why Bother with All This Fuss?

Sure, the initial setup takes a few extra minutes compared to straight-up copy-pasting, but ongoing maintenance is a breeze, with two big upsides:

Benefit 1: Updates only once. Since all projects link to the same original via soft links, when the open-source project drops an update, I just pull the latest code in ~/GitHub/baoyu-skills, and every project using that Skill instantly gets the new version.

Benefit 2: Bug fixes feed right back. Say I'm using the comic Skill in my writing project to draw comics and spot an issue—I just have the Agent fix it on the spot. Since it's a soft link, the Agent's tweaking the original in the repo (~/GitHub/baoyu-skills/skills/baoyu-comic), so I can commit the fix straight to the open-source project, basically tossing in a free contribution to the community.

legacy.full_text:
分享一下我管理 Skills 的方式，偏极客风格，不一定适合所有人，但可以给大家提供一个思路。

一、Skills 只装在项目里，不装全局

Agent 的 Skills 可以装在全局（所有项目共享）或者项目内（只有当前项目能用）。我选择只装在项目内，最主要的原因是节约上下文空间。

Agent https://t.co/aFyaj6ciSM

result.text:
分享一下我管理 Skills 的方式，偏极客风格，不一定适合所有人，但可以给大家提供一个思路。

一、Skills 只装在项目里，不装全局

Agent 的 Skills 可以装在全局（所有项目共享）或者项目内（只有当前项目能用）。我选择只装在项目内，最主要的原因是节约上下文空间。

Agent 在工作时有一个上下文窗口，你可以把它想象成 Claude 的工作台——台面大小是有限的。虽然 Skill 默认只会加载名称、描述等摘要信息（不会把完整内容全部摊开），但积少成多——全局装了几十个 Skill，光是这些摘要加在一起也会占掉不少工作台空间。而且一旦 Claude 判断某个 Skill 跟当前任务相关，就会把它的完整内容加载进来，全局 Skill 越多，被误触发的概率也越大，白白浪费空间。

只在项目内安装真正需要的 Skills，工作台上就只摆当前用得到的资料，把宝贵的空间留给更重要的内容，Claude 干活也更高效。

二、用软链接来安装 Skills

这是我管理方式的核心，先解释一下什么是软链接。

你可以把软链接理解成 Windows 的快捷方式——文件本体只有一份，但你可以在很多地方创建快捷方式指向它。改了本体，所有快捷方式指向的内容都会同步变化。

我的具体做法分三步：

第一步：把开源 Skills 项目下载到统一的目录

我在电脑上有一个专门存放 GitHub 项目的文件夹 ~/GitHub，所有下载的开源项目都放在这里面，比如：

~/GitHub/baoyu-skills      ← 存放各种 Skills 的开源项目
~/GitHub/baoyu-design      ← 另一个开源项目

这个文件夹就像一个仓库，所有 Skills 的原件都保存在这里。

第二步：在自己的项目中创建软链接

假设我有一个写作项目 ~/GitHub/baoyu-writing，里面需要用到好几个 Skills。我不会把 Skills 复制进来，而是创建软链接，让项目指向仓库里的原件：

项目内的路径                         →  实际指向的位置（原件）
.agents/skills/baoyu-comic          →  ~/GitHub/baoyu-skills/skills/baoyu-comic
.agents/skills/baoyu-design         →  ~/GitHub/baoyu-design/skills/baoyu-design

第三步：给 Claude Code 建一个入口

最后再创建一个软链接，让 Claude Code 能找到这些 Skills：

.claude/skills  →  .agents/skills

这样 Claude Code 就能顺着这条链找到所有需要的 Skills 了。

三、不用记命令，让 Agent 帮你干

看到这里你可能会想：软链接的命令我记不住怎么办？

完全不用记。直接用自然语言告诉 Codex/Claude Code 你要做什么就行了，比如：

> 帮我把 ~/GitHub/baoyu-skills/skills/baoyu-comic 软链接到 .agents/skills/baoyu-comic

甚至更简单：

帮我把 baoyu-skills 项目里的 baoyu-comic 这个 skill 链接到当前项目

Agent 会自动帮你创建软链接，后续的维护、添加、删除也都可以交给它。你只需要说清楚要把哪个 Skill 链到哪，剩下的脏活累活让 Agent 干就好。

四、为什么值得这么折腾？

初次设置确实比直接复制粘贴多花几分钟，但后续维护特别省心，主要有两个好处：

好处一：更新只需一次。因为所有项目都是通过软链接指向同一份原件的，所以当开源项目有更新时，我只需要去 ~/GitHub/baoyu-skills 拉取最新代码，所有用到这个 Skill 的项目就自动变成最新版了。

好处二：修了 bug 可以直接反哺。比如我在写作项目里用漫画 Skill 画漫画时发现了一个问题，直接让 Agent 修复就好。因为是软链接，Agent 修改的其实是仓库里的原件（~/GitHub/baoyu-skills/skills/baoyu-comic），我可以直接把修复提交到开源项目，相当于顺手给开源社区做了贡献。

For Skills updates, it's best to follow Git. They're usually open-source projects, and even if not, you can manage them with a private Git repo. To update, just git pull from the corresponding project; for a specific version, git checkout it.
You don't need to do this yourself
data.translation:
For Skills updates, it's best to follow Git. They're usually open-source projects, and even if not, you can manage them with a private Git repo. To update, just git pull from the corresponding project; for a specific version, git checkout it.
You don't need to do this yourself either—you can set up a scheduled task in Codex/CC to handle it automatically.
https://t.co/ttv3VOTSQe

这事你也不用自己做，可以在 Codex/CC 里面搞个定时任务，自动做

data.preview_translation:
Skill is something that needs to be iterated on while using it, which is why I use a soft link pointing to the same location—it's for easy updates, because no matter which project I discover an issue in, I can directly update the original location.

But if it's someone else's
data.translation:
Skill is something that needs to be iterated on while using it, which is why I use a soft link pointing to the same location—it's for easy updates, because no matter which project I discover an issue in, I can directly update the original location.

But if it's someone else's Skill, you need to update it frequently; it's best to fork a copy, so any modifications are in your local version, and you can periodically sync with upstream.
https://t.co/wP94ogxaXN

legacy.full_text:
Skill 就是需要一边用一边迭代的，这就是为什么我是用软链接指向同一位置，就是为了方便更新，因为无论在哪个项目中我发现问题，都可以直接更新原始位置。

但如果是别人的 Skill，你需要经常更新，最好 fork 一份，这样有修改都在你本地，也可以定期和upstream同步
https://t.co/wP94ogxaXN

data.preview_translation:
Teacher Baoyu, I have a question. During my usage process, I often need to fine-tune the original skill to adapt it to the specific requirements of my project.

In such cases, my current approach is to directly pull the skill into the global scope. During usage, if I find
data.translation:
Teacher Baoyu, I have a question. During my usage process, I often need to fine-tune the original skill to adapt it to the specific requirements of my project.

In such cases, my current approach is to directly pull the skill into the global scope. During usage, if I find something that needs adjustment, I directly import the skill into the project for adjustment and use, and the original skill gets discarded right away.

I've already had to custom-redefine and handle around ten skills this way. Is this a reasonable way to use skills?

legacy.full_text:
@dotey 宝玉老师我有个疑问，我在使用过程中经常会去微调下原skill，以适配我的项目的特定需求的能力。
这种情况我目前的做法是直接拉skill到全局，使用过程中发现要调整，直接把skill引入到项目中去调整使用，原有的skill直接就干掉了。

data.preview_translation:
CC and Codex are both top-tier Agent tools, but the same skill plays a completely different role in the two tools.

The same writing-type skill, when you want to do content creation, CC will have a better effect than Codex, after all, CC's aesthetics have always been on point.
data.translation:
CC and Codex are both top-tier Agent tools, but the same skill plays a completely different role in the two tools.

The same writing-type skill, when you want to do content creation, CC will have a better effect than Codex, after all, CC's aesthetics have always been on point.
But when it comes to writing workflow documents, Codex will be much better than CC.

Often, one skill has to be installed twice in both pieces of software, and later on, as the skills pile up, it all looks messy.

To solve this hassle, I directly made a skill manager.
Source code link in the comments👇

但是想做工作流文档的写作codex又会比CC好很多

legacy.full_text:
@dotey 参考宝玉老师的思路，做了个跨 Agent 的 skill 管理版：

data.preview_translation:
Teacher Baoyu, I have a question. During my usage process, I often need to fine-tune the original skill to adapt it to the specific requirements of my project.

In such cases, my current approach is to directly pull the skill into the global scope. During usage, if I find
data.translation:
Teacher Baoyu, I have a question. During my usage process, I often need to fine-tune the original skill to adapt it to the specific requirements of my project.

In such cases, my current approach is to directly pull the skill into the global scope. During usage, if I find something that needs adjustment, I directly import the skill into the project for adjustment and use, and the original skill gets discarded right away.

I've already had to custom-redefine and handle around ten skills this way. Is this a reasonable way to use skills?

legacy.full_text:
@dotey 宝玉老师我有个疑问，我在使用过程中经常会去微调下原skill，以适配我的项目的特定需求的能力。
这种情况我目前的做法是直接拉skill到全局，使用过程中发现要调整，直接把skill引入到项目中去调整使用，原有的skill直接就干掉了。

result.text:
宝玉老师我有个疑问，我在使用过程中经常会去微调下原skill，以适配我的项目的特定需求的能力。
这种情况我目前的做法是直接拉skill到全局，使用过程中发现要调整，直接把skill引入到项目中去调整使用，原有的skill直接就干掉了。
目前已经重新自定义处理了十来个skill了，这样操作是skill的合理的使用方式么

data.preview_translation: I've found this soft link setup really useful, and I'd like to chase down a collaboration scenario: when working solo across multiple projects, "change once, update everywhere" is a pure win, but in team collaboration, when others clone your project, they end up with a dead link
data.translation: I've found this soft link setup really useful, and I'd like to chase down a collaboration scenario: when working solo across multiple projects, "change once, update everywhere" is a pure win, but in team collaboration, when others clone your project, they end up with a dead link pointing to your local ~/GitHub. How do you handle that—is it by agreeing on a consistent repo structure for everyone, or by just copying key skills straight into the project and only using soft links for the ones you tweak often? I've been swinging back and forth between "reuse" and "portability" forever.

legacy.full_text: @dotey 这套软链方案我很受用，想追一个协作场景：单人多项目时”改一次处处更新”是纯优点，但多人协作时，别人 clone 你的项目拿到的是个指向你本地 ~/GitHub 的死链。你是怎么处理的——是约定大家仓库结构一致，还是关键 skill 干脆复制进项目、只对自己常改的用软链？我一直在”复用”和”可移植”之间摇摆。

data.preview_translation: Managing it this way is indeed convenient in a multi-agent setup. However, installing skills this way might be a bit more troublesome, as you'll need to have the agent download it to the GitHub directory first and then create a soft link. Otherwise, using a command like `npx add`
data.translation: Managing it this way is indeed convenient in a multi-agent setup. However, installing skills this way might be a bit more troublesome, as you'll need to have the agent download it to the GitHub directory first and then create a soft link. Otherwise, using a command like `npx add` directly installs it to the global skill directory.

legacy.full_text: @dotey 多 agent 情况下这样管理确实方便。不过这样安装 skill 的时候可能稍微麻烦点，需要让 agent 先下载到 github 目录，然后创建软连接。否则 直接使用 npx add 这种指令就直接给装到 skill全局目录下了
